### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-19
@mimiba - question...if your hbd..1 is just your xp system os, then why do you need to mount it?
also, you could try this command to mount since i noticed there are non-english characters in your output.. ```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac1 /media/Windows -o locale=hu_HU.utf8
``` this makes filenames with non-english characters visible and might be your problem. of course that is assuming from your output that there are non-english character filenames.

also if that doesnt work try this:```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac1 /media/Windows -o locale=hu_HU.utf8 errors=recover defaults
```

also i noticed that your win95 extended partition contains both your ntfs partition, linux swap and ext3. This may be the issue itself. Since your lucky and seemed to have ordered your ntfs partion at the very end of the drive it will be simple to fix.  Grab that annoying win98 boot disk with fdisk on it, and repartition your drive to the following layout:```
                &#25152;&#29992;&#35037;&#32622; Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac1   *           1        2550    20482843+   7  HPFS/NTFS
/dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac2            2551       7267   140343840    f  W95 Ext'd (LBA)
/dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac6            2551        2673      987966   82  Linux swap / Solaris
/dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac7   *        2674        7267    36901273+  83  Linux
/dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac8            7268       20022   (w/e the blocks are)    f  Extended LBA number 2
/dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac9            7268       20022   102454506    7  HPFS/NTFS
```
where hac8 is another extended partition, hac9 is the logical partition on the new extended partition. The first extended partition remains, simply removing the ntfs partition, leaving the two logical linux and swap partitions untouched.
you should be able to accomplish this without erasing any data on your linux partiton, but your ntfs partition will most likely be smoked. So youll need to make a backup of anything you want to keep. And just to be safe, if you take this course of action, make a back up of everything you want to keep just in case!

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
@atmartin - hmm, since you are hibernating windows and switching to linux, this may be what is causing the problem.  whenever ntfs partitions are mounted, a logfile is created and saved with changes made about the data, etc. however, since your only hibernating it seems that xp is not writing and closing that logfile, leaving linux unable to access it since its still in use. Have you tried booting in linux first, hibernating, and booting into xp, then hibernating back into linux when you want?

im assuming you want to be able to write to your ntfs partition since your here, so the ro option is completely useless to you. At the moment, this is all that i can think of to solve the problem. If i find something ill be sure to get back asap if the above doesn't work.
I personally dislike hibernating greatly after an incident before i used linux, where i lost around 80gb of personal documents and music after trying to wake my computer from hibernation and getting a bsod x.x

also when you use mount and pmount, make sure that after your device (/dev/sda1) that you have a mount point (like /media/windows or /media/whatever)

---

### Post by szaka on 2007-06-19
> **mimiba said:**
> But follows the problem, when I use the same method to mount /dev/mapper/nvidia_hbdhjhac5
It failed..........

You weren't mounting with ntfs-3g but with the kernel NTFS driver.  Change the 'ntfs' file system type to 'ntfs-3g' in your fstab.

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-19
thanks szaka, i made a mistake when put that code! ill fix it now :P:P sorry about that :P

@atmartin - the old logfile could definetly still be there, just corrupted or incorrectly saved.

an elaboration hmm.. well here is an example of what i meant:```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/ntfspartition -o errors=recover defaults
```
where "*-t ntfs-3g*" declares the filesystem type. we declare this using the ntfs-3g tools
.........."*/dev/sda1*" is your partition [note!: /dev/sda is your actual hardrive, while the ending number (the 1 in /dev/sda1) represents your partitions]
.........."*/media/ntfspartition*" can be any mount point. meaning this is where you would navigate using your file manager to view your files.
..........."*-o errors=recover defaults*" is just some extra options. "*errors=recover*" tries to recover/fix the partition if any errors occur when trying to mount, "*defaults*" allows a non-root user to read/write to the drive. Read the man page to find out more about those: "*man mount*"

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-20
hmm, well, after reading ntfs-3g man page, it seems the errors option is not listed, so it may not be supported. use this instead:```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda /media/western_digital -o defaults
```
if that fails, This is kinda risky, but try adding "*force*" right after defaults. This will force a mount even if there are errors on the drive. this will clean the logfile, but if something goes awry, that would be really bad...(you might loose everything on the partition so be warned when using the force option!)

btw, we are using the mount device option. simply putting mount lists all the mounted devices on your system, mount -a remounts all the devices on your system without restart, and mount directory can be used to rename or move the mount location of a device :P but note, only "mount" and "mount -a" from what i just said are actual commands. "mount device" "mount directory" are used to describe the kind of mount, trying to use those two commands in the terminal will result in an error :P

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-20
since you have it all backed up.. do the force option:
```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda /media/western_digital -o defaults locale=hu_HU.utf8 force
```
I added the force option which will force even though the logfile is not clean. I also added a locale option which will ensure that all your files are readable in case you have some non-english characters.

hope this works!

@mimiba - you could also use your live cd for ubuntu and use gparted. Its under system>administration, or if its not there... applications>system tools>partition editor
this will compensate for raid0 i believe, unless im mistaken.  Although i can't guarantee that it wont mess up your previous partitions... so make a backup! In the end you should have a layout in a more user friendly view similar to:```
Primary - Windows i believe you had (bootable)
Extended 1
----Logical 1 - Linux Swap
----Logical 2 - Linux ext3 (bootable)
Extended 2
----Logical 3 - ntfs partition
```hope that is easier to understand :P sometimes I get a little carried away :P

Edit: if you would like to use fdisk instead, or learn about it a bit, i found this post about repartitioning, although its a little technical, you be warned :P [link.](http://www.linuxhomenetworking.com/wiki/index.php/Quick_HOWTO_:_Ch26_:_Linux_Software_RAID#RAID_0)

---

### Post by szaka on 2007-06-20
> do the force option:
```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda /media/western_digital -o defaults locale=hu_HU.utf8 force
```
I added the force option which will force even though the logfile is not clean. I also added a locale option which will ensure that all your files are readable in case you have some non-english characters.

The correct syntax is 
```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda /media/western_digital -o locale=hu_HU.utf8,force
```
and the 'force' option will only work in your case if you use at least ntfs-3g version  1.417. Otherwise you will have the same "logfile unclean" error message.

### Post by szaka on 2007-06-20
> **atmartin50 said:**
> I'm really at a loss as to what to do next. All of my USB devices are currently unusable. Would a re-install fix this problem?

You don't need to reinstall. Your ntfs-3g version is old. You don't have at least version 1.417. Your ntfsfix is also old.

Boot Windows then click the 'Safely Remove Hardware' icon in the Windows taskbar notification area before disconnecting your external device.

Or upgrade ntfs-3g to the latest version, currently 1.616, and repeat the same command.

---

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-20
hm, doesnt matter on my system whether there are commas separating the options or not :P
although i guess i shouldnt take that for granted :P thanks again szaka.

follow what szaka said about removing the hd in windows.

if you've recently installed, like i have, you should have 1.516, although this may not be the case with feisty. try upgrading first and see if you get at least the version i am using```
sudo apt-get upgrade
sudo apt-get install ntfs-3g libntfs-3g2 ntfsprogs
``` either way we can still install from source the newest version. I am running 1.516 of ntfs-3g and 1.13 of ntfsprogs. to find your version, in the terminal type "*man ntfs-3g*" and "*man ntfsprogs*"and scroll to the very bottom and it will tell you its version.

to install from source the newest ntfs-3g:
to do this we need to make sure that we have the newest fuse module:```
sudo apt-get upgrade
sudo apt-get install fuse-utils libfuse2
```
if it says the most recent version is installed thats good :P

download the **[source](http://www.ntfs-3g.org/ntfs-3g-1.616.tgz)**.
extract it to your home directory (/home/yourusername -  when you go to Places>home in your menu bar this is where it takes you :)). Configure and install. I'm assuming you saved this file in your home directory!```
tar xvfz ./ntfs-3g-1.616.tgz
cd ./ntfs-3g-1.616
sudo ./configure
sudo make
sudo make install
```

to upgrade your ntfsprogs from source:
download the **[source ](http://superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/linux-ntfs/ntfsprogs-1.13.1.tar.gz)** and save in home directory like before:```
tar xvfz ntfsprogs-1.13.1.tar.gz
cd ./ntfsprogs-1.13.1
sudo ./configure
sudo make
sudo make install
```

After completing this you will have the newest version of ntfs-3g from source.
then run the previous code again:```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda /media/western_digital -o defaults,force
```

@mimiba - welcome! :D

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-21
ah :P cool cool

these are probably already isntalled by default, but lets just double check:```
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade && sudo apt-get install gcc g++ cpp g77 gobjc gpc libstdc++2.10-glibc2.2 libgcc1 pkg-config kernel-package linux-headers-generic linux-libc-dev gawk

```
there are other compilers in there, like fortran, objective c, and a pascal. although they are not useful for this, i went ahead and included them just in case you end up using them in the future. :P also your headers will be installed, although search synaptic for "linux headers" and install all the main ones these linux headers are important, for the rest of the process, but the generic ones should work, although try to get your version for the best results
to find your kernel version:```
uname -r
``` that is the header version you need to look for ;) it will be something similar to: *"linux-headers-2.6.22-6-generic"*

now try running the last 3 commands. but modified:```
sudo ./configure --with-kernel
sudo make
sudo make install
``` if you get an error about fuse needing to be 2.6 or greater... your gonna have to install fuse from source... yeah. i know bleh x.x :P but thats why im here to help :P

same process... download **[source](http://sourceforge.net/project/showfiles.php?group_id=121684&package_id=132802&release_id=504573)**
unpack in home directory, cd to directory
```
sudo ./configure --enable-kernel-module
sudo make
sudo make install
su -c 'rmmod fuse'
su -c 'ldconfig'
```
then try the other two again :P

hey at least your learning and getting more comfortable with the terminal :D it is definitely your friend :D

Edit: found this forum that also goes through this same process (eventhough it is opensuse it is the same process pretty much :P): **[link](http://forum.ntfs-3g.org/viewtopic.php?t=21)**

Edit2: hahah just noticed something :P szaka, you're just everywhere aren't you? :P > ...Enjoy ntfs-3g, and don't forget to thank szaka...

---

@mimiba - you could also use your live cd for ubuntu and use gparted. Its under system>administration, or if its not there... applications>system tools>partition editor
this will compensate for raid0 i believe, unless im mistaken.  Although i can't guarantee that it wont mess up your previous partitions... so make a backup! In the end you should have a layout in a more user friendly view similar to:```

Hi platinummonkey:

I've adjust the partition tabel according to your suggestion, except that I could not creat a secondary extension partition, so I back up the files in logical ntfs partition, and make it a primary one, by using linux fdisk..

the partition table now look like this one: 

```

Primary 1 -- Windows XP system disk
Extended
--- Logical 1 - Linux swap
--- Logical 2 - Linux ext3 
Primary 2 -- ntfs partition (Datas)

```

I have to back up the data in the NTFS partition while making the change. (because after change the patition table, the XP recognized the partition as a new one)
Now, I can use the primary 2 partition in Ubuntu. Thanks your suggestion.

@atmartin - ah, well instead of the su -c ...  use: ```
sudo su
rmmod fuse
ldconfig

```
you'll want to close that terminal window and open a new one :P because "sudo su" puts you in the root terminal ;) (so if someone tells you to become root before a set of commands, just use "sudo su" ;))

and yeah > ? Do you mean these commands?... yeah i meant those :P if you've already extracted them, then the first part of each of those is uneccessary just run once youve cd'd into the directory:```
./configure --with-kernel
sudo make
sudo make install
``` for both ntfs-3g and ntfs-progs

that should work :P if you get an error with the first command, try removing the "--with-kernel" part ;)


---------------------------------------------------------------------
@mr. snowflake - have you tried installing the manual way?, read that and try with ntfs-3g, and also check out from page ~175 till now, if some trouble happens :P

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-24
@atmartin - thanks! been out of town the last couple days :P anyways, yeah most of the time  you can delete those extracted files. but any new folders in your home dir or whereever else it may have created some, do not delete :P  my installation was unnaffected with the deletion. Sometimes when you compile from source, the program resides from the building dir and you  have to filter out the ones you dont need, other times it creates the necessary files elsewhere. all three should be fine if you delete the files. try moving them to the trash, reboot. if something goes awry then move them back. no harm no foul. :P

as for the error msg. try running the following command when your drive is mounted:```
sudo  chown -hR your_username:your_group /media/western_digital
``` note: this will probably take a few minutes :P

-----------------------------------------------------
@klrrider - try installing from source. but note, all those packages listed are required by ntfs-config to work properly. you can install them by:```
sudo apt-get install ntfs-config -f
```
and if it still gives you an error msg run:```
sudo apt-get install package-name -f
``` for each package. replace *package-name* with whatever the package name is, and you can put more than one on that single command. just make sure they appear before the *-f*


-----------------------------------------------------
@aseembehl - doesnt seem your fuse is setup right. so try reinstalling fuse.  read a few posts back by me on how to do this from source. ;) after that, everything should work.

---

### Post by atmartin50 on 2007-06-24
Thanks yet again, platinummonkey!

This may be a dumb question, but in the command

> sudo  chown -hR your_username:your_group /media/western_digital

what is my group? I don't think I've run into that before.

Muchos gracias!

---

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-24
on default, your group is usually just your username too if you did the graphical install. which im sure you did :P but if you created a different group or called it something else, it needs to be that. to check what group, go into your home folder. choose any random file that you put there. right click>properties go to permissions tab. and it will tell you what group.
groups can be used to "group" ;) together regular users with similar permissions. so if you had 25 users, and 5 groups. the differend groups could all have different permissions and abilities. while the root user is under the root group and the root user has all the power :P

---

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-25
@atmartin - ah, this issue could be to the fact that when you used the mount command, you didnt specify non-root users the ability to r/w and unmount abilities. try using: ```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/western_digital -o defaults
```
the defaults option includes several diffenent options used including non-root users access to r/w and to unmount the drive.

as for the automounting, this is a whole different issue. mine seems to automount on its own with ntfs-3g without any extra configuration. i did find this [link](http://forum.ntfs-3g.org/viewtopic.php?t=416) for more information
you may be able to successfully automount with ntfs-config ;) (although r/w issues may occur again x.x)

-----------------------------------------
@hairyhi - try mounting with: ```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /whatever/mount/point/you/want -n -o defaults
```
the *"-n"* signal disbles the writing to a mtab file. this may or may not solve the issue.
editing the file by hand is not suggested, and since the file is constantly being overwritten, any hand editing will soon be overwritten anyways :P

---

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-26
use this to find your uid and gid information:```
id *username*
```replacing the username with your non-root username that you would like to r/w with the drive.

try ```
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /mount/point -o rw,uid=youruid,gid=yourgid,user_allow_other
```substitting youruid and yourgid with your actuall id numbers :P

hopefully this will work :P

@platinummonkey

> @aseembehl - doesnt seem your fuse is setup right. so try reinstalling fuse. read a few posts back by me on how to do this from source.  after that, everything should work.

I tried reinstalling fuse from source by the method mentioned(see below) by you in this thread

> if you get an error about fuse needing to be 2.6 or greater... your gonna have to install fuse from source... yeah. i know bleh x.x :P but thats why im here to help :P

same process... download source
unpack in home directory, cd to directory

```
sudo ./configure --enable-kernel-module
sudo make
sudo make install
su -c 'rmmod fuse'
su -c 'ldconfig'
```



But I still get  the same error of 
```
/sbin/mount.ntfs-3g: /usr/lib/libfuse.so.2: version `FUSE_2.6' not found (required by sbin/mount.ntfs-3g)
``` with ```
sudo mount -a
```

So I tried another method for installing fuse(also mentioned in this thread)

```
1. You'll need first to get the source of the new fuse module :

sudo apt-get install fuse-source module-assistant

2. Then we will build and install the new module for your running kernel :

sudo module-assistant auto-install fuse

module-assistant will take care of all the dependency, and will do everything
automatically. You'll probably need to have a working internet connection.

3. Finaly, you'll need to load it. Two ways :

- You can reboot your cumputer now, and the new module should be load.
- If you don't want to reboot, first unmount all partitions that currently use ntfs-3g,
then restart fuse :

sudo modprobe -r fuse
sudo modprobe fuse
```

Even this doesn't work?? I get the same error.

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-28
@aseembehl - we should check to see if fuse is properly installed. make sure that /dev/fuse exists! [link from quoted text:](http://fuse.sourceforge.net/wiki/index.php/FAQ)[quote=fuse.sourceforge.net]What do I do if /dev/fuse does not exist?

Maybe the FUSE module is not loaded. As root, try:

modprobe fuse

If nothing changes, as root run:

mknod /dev/fuse c 10 229

What do I do if you don't have access to /dev/fuse?

As root run:

chmod o+rw /dev/fuse

Remember that this will allow ordinary users to mount their own user space filesystems.

If that's not what you want then use different permissions.[/quote]
if fuse is properly installed, then it seems your ntfs-3g installation has not been able to recognize this yet. If you installed from source. go back to the source directory that you used to install with and type: ```
sudo make uninstall
``` and then reinstall from source again using the whole ```
./configure
sudo make
sudo make install
```

if you installed from the repos, then just ```
sudo apt-get install ntfs-3g libntfs-3g2 libntfs-3g-dev --reinstall
``` if you didnt have one of those packages installed in the first place, then remove it from that command :P

---------------------------------------------
@green-12 - as far as i know, yes :P try [ntfs-3g.org](http://www.ntfs-3g.org/) ;)

--------------------------------------------
@atmartin and hairyhi - hm, well something that might work for you both -- ntfs-config should do the auto mounting for you with user privileges, so no mount commands should be needed. ```
sudo apt-get install pmount ntfs-config -f
```ntfs-config depends on pmount, so it is necessary. here is the [homepage of ntfs-config](http://flomertens.free.fr/ntfs-config/index.html) if you would like to dl the source and install from there instead :P

------------------------------------------
@kblo - you say that it happens at the exact same spot on the media... sounds like it is a bit corrupted...  make sure that when you want to disconnect the device in whatever OS that you use, that you umount it before hand. Not doing so can cause various amounts of corruption.

---------------------------------------------
@ethana2 - since it is 3rd party, i wouldn't expect it to come pre-installed on any distrobutions in the near future; although, it is possible. As far as I know, unless im mistaken, there are no distro's at the moment that come pre-installed with ntfs-3g; although, quite a few have pre-compiled binaries available for easy installation.

-------------------------------------------
phew :P i think i answered everyone :P

### Post by platinummonkey on 2007-06-29
@ aseembehl - thx! np! glad its working for you now! :D:D

----------------------------------------------------
@kblo - i think i know what your problem is.  I want you to open up your computer when it is off! and double check the jumper pins on your hardrive. If you are using CSEL (Cable Select) on either both or one, this could be the issue right here. It is not good practice to do this. Make sure one is on master and one is on slave using your cable ribbon as a guide. Usually i find that the blue end of the cable goes into the mobo, the grey end is the slave, and the black end is the master. You may need to reconfigure ;), but once you have rearranged it should boot up just fine without this switching. [link with pictures on it in case you dont know](http://www.helpwithpcs.com/upgrading/install-hard-drive.htm)

---

so, let's see what goes on for next boots...

### Post by theDaveTheRave on 2007-07-11
YIPEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEE.

fully funcitoning swap and shared partitions.

All OK and being used, Great stuff.

Thanks for everyones help on this forum. I'll put together that brief howto now I think.

One quick question, since I have mangaged to get this to work I have lost functionality of my DVD playback (I use VLC), does anyone have a reason as to why this might be??

I need to test for backward compatibility an try with the "old" fstab I saved, to see if it comes back.

Battaz.

From what people are saying it seems that most keyboard have the "|" combined as the shift function on the "\" key. You will also find it as part of the ASCII codes (just google ASCII).

The "|" is often called the "pipe" (I'm sure that people will correct me if I'm wrong) and I believe that it enable you to write 2 commands on the same line to be executed consecutively, sometimes you want the result of a command (eg dmesg) to be "piped" straight into a file, rather than returning the result to screen. Don't ask me how this is done as I have never been able to get it to function as it should. The whole procedure is part of the "GREP" command - you'll have to look it up in the forums / tutorials, and is part of the niceness of Linux.

Try doing the <dmesg> command in a terminal, and the result is huge. Witht eh "Pipe" and the GREP you can reduce this to the information that you are concerned with - as I said before I really don't understand how it works, and can't seem to get it to function in a satisfactory manner - but I'm sure I'm just doing something wrong!

Hope that helps.

Dave

### Post by maestrobwh1 on 2007-07-12
Perhaps it is the "fuse" issue.  I am thinking the kernel that is with feisty is fine, whereas the one with Edgy is lacking the up to date item.  I know there are fuse-utils... but I know nothign to very little about compiling things , I have about a 25% success rate of compiling programs that will run in general so I am not optimistic on that.  I don't need to write to my NTFS on my laptop, so much as read it, so therefore the default ubuntu setup of fstab was probably sufficient. I'll leave the ntfs-3g there and will just avoid writing to it until I do further research.

### Post by Seeker84 on 2007-07-12
It's turns out that I had ntfs-3g installed in my distro (Ubuntu Ultimate 1.3, edgy based) but ntfs-config was not installed.  I installed it and everything is happy doory.

Thanks for all your help guys.

Also note, I didn't have to edit the fstab file.

---

### Post by maestrobwh1 on 2007-07-12
For the life of me, I just could not get anything to work without warnings on my edgy machine... so I went to [http://fuse.sourceforge.net/](http://fuse.sourceforge.net/) and downloaded the latest file.  I uncompressed it.
Then I did the usual 
./configure
make
sudo make install.  

Usually I have issues when installing from source.

That went well... and I was on a roll, so I went to the ntfs-3g site, downloaded the latest tar and did the same thing.  I removed "force" from my fstab entry, rebooted, and alas, the drive is automatically mounted and readable.  If I mount or unmount from terminal, the warnings are gone:)

### Post by maestrobwh1 on 2007-07-13
Since I have edgy on my laptop and fieity on my desktop, and since I installed ntfs-3g on my feisty machine first, I though my laptop would be a no brainer. ### Post by szaka on 2007-07-20
> **heyilikeyoursweater said:**
> I get this error after doing mount -a:
mount: unknown filesystem type 'ntfs-fuse'
The file system type should be ntfs-3g not ntfs-fuse in /etc/fstab if you plan to use the NTFS-3G driver.

### Post by ethana2 on 2007-08-26
Just open synaptic, search for ntfs-3g, select it, click apply.

Give you a GUI and everything, under Applications->System tools.

You don't have to touch the terminal.

That's how I finally did it.  --and I wish someone told me about that before I ended up thinking it was all complicated, too ;(  Maybe somewhere they did and I missed it...

### Post by szaka on 2007-09-13
> **prince_niceguy said:**
> I am having some issues iwth ntfs-3g. I have been using this for quite long now and everything works fine except for one thing. some time when I delete the files permanently they get renamed to

.fuse_hiddenXXXXXXX where XXXX stands for some number.

After this happend no matter what I do I cannot get rid of these files. every time I delete them they assume some other number but size remains the same.

I can go to terminal and clear them using echo but I am not able to delete them any way.

Any idea what this could be...???
The files/directories are in use by some software hence they can't be removed **immediately**. As soon as you exit those softwares or reboot then these .fuse_hiddenXXXXX files will be gone/deleted automatically.

### Post by kingofpain on 2007-09-19
Vampire, don't run ntfs-config. Configure it manually (modify /etc/fstab). It's easy and most importantly, it works.
It's obvious, your problem is from the configuration generated by ntfs-config.

P.S.: I hope you backed up your /etc/fstab before running ntfs-config...

### Post by vampire666eng on 2007-09-19
Ok...some progress.

I changed in the /etc/fstab
this (example for one partition):
```
UUID=E0ECE3E5ECE3B3C6 /media/hdb1     ntfs-3g     defaults,locale=en_US.utf8   0    0
```

to this(example for one partition):
```
UUID=E0ECE3E5ECE3B3C6 /media/hdb1     ntfs-3g     nls=utf8,umask=0222 0 0
```

And finally I see all the partition in the Computer nautilus...but I can't access them. I have to run the "sudo mount" -a command and the drives are finally accessible.

### Post by kingofpain on 2007-09-20
Vampire, you said you have a backup of your /etc/fstab.
A line in that file looks like this:
```
# /dev/sda1
UUID=B2B8AC11B8ABD261 /media/sda1     ntfs    	 defaults,nls=utf8,umask=007,gid=46 0       1
```

Now, you have to change it to look like this:
```
# /dev/sda1
UUID=B2B8AC11B8ABD261 /media/sda1     ntfs-3g    defaults,nls=utf8,gid=46 	    0       0
```

So, you have to modify **only**: 
1. ntfs-3g instead of ntfs
2. delete that umask thing
3. last filelds must to be set to 0 0

And that's it. Now it should work. I works fine for me.

 mike, I don't know, those UUID's make me feel dizzy. Try wrinting normal, old style lines in /etc/fstab.
I mean, instead of those UUID's, write something like this:
/dev/sda2  /media/sda2 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_US.utf8   0    0

Oh, and take a look at ubuntu feisty guide ( [http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu:F...d_write_access](http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu:F...d_write_access) ) to see how /etc/fstab should look.

Good luck!

### Post by Dragonborn64 on 2007-09-23
Hey-
I downloaded the auto-config but forgot to run it before my computer was rebooted.......... when I tried to mount, it came up with an error........... 

~$ **sudo mount -a**
Password:
$LogFile indicates unclean shutdown (0, 0)
Failed to mount '/dev/sda1': Operation not supported
Mount is denied because NTFS logfile is unclean. Choose one action:
   Boot Windows and shutdown it cleanly, or if you have a removable
   device then click the 'Safely Remove Hardware' icon in the Windows
   taskbar notification area before disconnecting it.
Or
   Run ntfsfix version 1.13.1 on Linux unless you have Vista.
Or
   Mount the NTFS volume with the 'ro' option in read-only mode.

Since I don't have a dual boot, and I wanted to write to my external hard drive, I ran ntfsfix ..........

**ntfsfix /dev/sda1**
Mounting volume... FAILED
Attempting to correct errors... 
Processing $MFT and $MFTMirr...
Reading $MFT... OK
Reading $MFTMirr... OK
Comparing $MFTMirr to $MFT... OK
Processing of $MFT and $MFTMirr completed successfully.
Setting required flags on partition... OK
Going to empty the journal ($LogFile)... OK
NTFS volume version is 3.1.
NTFS partition /dev/sda1 was processed successfully.

after this I again tried to mount the hard drive and I saw this ..............

~$** sudo mount -a**
Volume is scheduled for check. Please boot into Windows TWICE, or
use the 'force' mount option. For example type on the command line:

    mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/sda1 -o force

Or add the option to the relevant row in the /etc/fstab file:

    /dev/sda1 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,force 0 0

So I chose to force the mount with .............

**mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/sda1 -o force** ......................

and so far my 120 Gig USB external hard drive is working great!!

It took a little bit of work and patience, but I can now use the hard drive I have been unable to use since I loaded Ubuntu onto my computer! And since I love to solve problems like this, which the new driver ntfs-3g has allowed me to do, the world is great again!!   :-P

Thanks a million!!

Dragon64

### Post by mssever on 2007-09-23
> **zhanglini said:**
>  /dev/sda1 /media/SimpleDrive ntfs-3g defaults[SIZE=3][COLOR=Red]**,**[/COLOR][/SIZE] 0 0
You have a stray comma in your fstab. That might be your problem. Also, it isn't a bad idea to specify your locale. For example, I'm in the US, so the line for me would be ```
/dev/sda1    /media/SimpleDrive    ntfs-3g    defaults,locale=en_US.utf8    0 0
```You can get a list of the locales installed on your machine with ```
locale -a
```Once you've fixed your fstab, type ```
sudo mount /media/SimpleDrive
```Post any errors you get. If there are no error messages, then you should be good to go. You can type ```
mount
``` and verify that your partition is actually in the list.

On another note, please surround the contents of files or error message in [code] tags. Such tags preserve the formatting and make your posts much easier to follow.

---

### Post by mssever on 2007-09-24
> **zhanglini said:**
> by the way the following is in /etc/fstab:
```
/dev/sda1 /media/SimpleDrive ntfs-3g defaults, locale=zh_CN.UTF-8 0 0
```

You can't have spaces between your mount options. Try this instead:```
 /dev/sda1 /media/SimpleDrive ntfs-3g defaults,locale=zh_CN.UTF-8 0 0
```

### Post by mssever on 2007-09-24
> **zhanglini said:**
> This is so frustrating!
When I tried this:```

 /dev/sda1 /media/SimpleDrive ntfs-3g defaults,force 0 0

```I got the following
```

hui@hui-laptop:~$ sudo mount /media/SimpleDrive
WARNING: Dirty volume mount was forced by the 'force' mount option.
fusermount: failed to access mountpoint /media/SimpleDrive: No such file or directory
FUSE mount point creation failed
Unmounting /dev/sda1 (SimpleDrive)

```
The mountpoint must exist and be an empty directory. ```
sudo mkdir /media/SimpleDrive
``` You should read [How to fstab]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=283131") for details.
> When I tried the following:
```
/dev/sda1 /media/SimpleDrive ntfs-3g defaults,locale=zh_CN.UTF-8 0 0
```I got this:
```
hui@hui-laptop:~$ sudo mount /media/SimpleDrive
Volume is scheduled for check. Please boot into Windows TWICE, or
use the 'force' mount option. For example type on the command line:

    mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/SimpleDrive -o force

Or add the option to the relevant row in the /etc/fstab file:

    /dev/sda1 /media/SimpleDrive ntfs-3g defaults,force 0 0

```You need to do as the message says and boot into Windows twice to cause Windows to repair the partition. Only use force as a last resort.

### Post by bodhi.zazen on 2007-10-12
looks like you need to make the mount point :

```
sudo mkdir /media/Orion
```

### Post by mssever on 2007-10-25
> **ospina.utp said:**
> err [http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ubuntu/](http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ubuntu/) edgy main main-all packages 404 was not found
404 errors mean that the requested file doesn't exist. It's a problem with that repo. Perhaps they dropped Edgy support.
> **computerjunkie said:**
> ok i got to the "fakeroot apt-get source -b ntfs-3g" step and there was no .deb package. 
<snip>
dpkg-checkbuilddeps: Unmet build dependencies: libfuse-dev (>= 2.6)
You're missing libfuse-dev, as the error message says. fuse-source and libfuse-dev are different packages. That's why they have different names.

### Post by sethno on 2007-11-10
How wouold these be set up in fstab?

Turn off the auto-mounting of other partitions or drives (NTFS, etc.) during boot.
Users are able to mount/umount those unmounted partitions or drives on demand.

Add another line using ntfs-3g for other partitions, also with
no auto-mounting and also with user abilities to mount/umount.

And...how is a read-only option added? 

If you are wondering... Yes, two different mounting options.
First using standard ntfs progs and second with ntfs-3g.

### Post by karim_eng on 2008-03-09
The Following way for Kubuntu

Go to your Konsole
First add the repositories in your sources list:

Command:
kdesu kate /etc/apt/sources.list

This command will open a file, add these lines to the bottom of the file, and click save then close the opened file

deb [http://flomertens.free.fr/ubuntu/](http://flomertens.free.fr/ubuntu/) edgy main main-all
deb [http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ubuntu/](http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ubuntu/) edgy main main-all
deb [http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/](http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/) edgy main main-all

Add the gpg key:
Command: (you dont have to write the following command if you are kubuntu 7.10)

wget [http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/givre_key.asc](http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/givre_key.asc) -O- | sudo apt-key add -

Update your system:

Command:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get upgrade

Now install the packages:

Command:
sudo apt-get install ntfs-3g ntfs-config

Now enter the ntfs config:

Command:
kdesu ntfs-config

Now you can read/write NTFS partitions!

### Post by sunseeker888 on 2008-03-16
/dev/<your partition>     /media/<mount point>     ntfs-3g     defaults,locale=en_US.utf8   0    0


HI guys I am a newbie

i know my partition sdb5 but don't have a clue what mount point

---

### Post by dark_phantom on 2008-03-19
> **sunseeker888 said:**
> /dev/<your partition>     /media/<mount point>     ntfs-3g     defaults,locale=en_US.utf8   0    0


HI guys I am a newbie 

what's media/<mount point>

i know my partition sdb5 but don't have a clue what mount point

Just refers to where you want it to be mounted.  For example, if you want to mount it to /media/windows then that's your mount point.  It will mount your /dev/sdb5 partition to /media/windows.

### Post by Alchera on 2008-04-22

7.10 has ntfs-3g inbuilt. All one has to do is create mount points and edit /etc/fstab accordingly or (in the case of KDE) run kcontrol and add the NTFS partitions via System Administration~Disk & File Systems.

### Post by bayvista on 2008-04-28
I have 5 ntfs-3g partitions all working well. However, when I delete any files, they just go into .trash.david and the space is not freed up. There does not seem to be any way of emptying the Recycle Bin. Currently, I access each partition in turn and delete .trash.david.

Any ideas on this one?

---

### Post by klytu on 2008-04-28
> **bayvista said:**
> I have 5 ntfs-3g partitions all working well. However, when I delete any files, they just go into .trash.david and the space is not freed up. There does not seem to be any way of emptying the Recycle Bin. Currently, I access each partition in turn and delete .trash.david.

Any ideas on this one?

In Nautilus go to Edit>Preferences>Behaviour and in the Trash section check the box "Include a Delete command that bypasses Trash". Then use delete to remove your files. Be very careful using that delete command - once files are deleted using it, there is no way that I am aware of to get them back!

### Post by sunspots on 2008-04-28
I have Kubuntu 8.04 on vmWare and as a guest OS on Windows XP.

I have mounted a USB drive that is on windows on Kubuntu and am able to see the files, but can't write files.

sudo mount -t cifs //192.168.x.y/Myshare /mnt/Myshare

Then I tried
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g /mnt/Myshare /mnt/MyshareNTFS

But get the error 
Error opening partition device: Is a directory
Failed to mount '/mnt/MyshareNTFS': Is a directory

---

### Post by sunspots on 2008-04-28
Well I slept on this issue and then went back to another of my posts on another forum and was given a hint.

So, I decided to go back to Windows and look at the sharing I established on the drive. Under the Share tab there is a checkbox against "Allow network users to change my files". Well it wasn't ticked.

Changed this to be ticked, went back to Kubuntu and it created the file successfully.

The post, pointed out that ntfs-3g is only usable for locally mounted drives and not across networks.

Maybe, this will be helpful to someone else or give them something else to check.

Thanks for the great HowTo, I know it will help me in the future when I set up my own server.

---

### Post by artships on 2008-04-29
> **klytu said:**
> once files are deleted using it, there is no way that I am aware of to get them back!
execute:
man ntfsundelete

Basically, it lists deleted files or, optionally, undelete's them.  Teh only tricky bit is that it uses hte device name ("/dev/hdb1") instead of the mout point ("/media/winxp").

### Post by mssever on 2008-06-18
> **aviborse said:**
> UPDATE MANAGER DESCRIPTION COPY

W: Failed to fetch [http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ub...gy/Release.gpg](http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ub...gy/Release.gpg) Could not resolve 'ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info'

W: Failed to fetch [http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ub...tion-en_IN.bz2](http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ub...tion-en_IN.bz2) Could not resolve 'ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info'

W: Failed to fetch [http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ub...tion-en_IN.bz2](http://ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info/ub...tion-en_IN.bz2) Could not resolve 'ntfs-3g.sitesweetsite.info'

W: Failed to fetch [http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/dist...86/Packages.gz](http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/dist...86/Packages.gz) 404 Not Found

W: Failed to fetch [http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/dist...86/Packages.gz](http://flomertens.keo.in/ubuntu/dist...86/Packages.gz) 404 Not Found

W: Some index files failed to download, they have been ignored, or old ones used instead.
First, this error message isn't serious. It only means that there were errors accessing specific repositories. Other repos (such as the official ones) aren't affected. Second, these repos have been down for a very long time, because ntfs-3g has been in the official repos for a long time. Beginning with either Feisty or Gutsy (I don't remember which), ntfs-3g has been installed by default. I know for a fact that in Hardy, you don't need to do anything to enable ntfs-3g.

The bottom line is that this thread is seriously outdated and should no longer be followed.

### Post by mssever on 2008-07-15
> **Daverobb said:**
> ```

/dev/sdc1 on /media/PHONE type vfat (rw,nosuid,nodev,uhelper=hal,shortname=mixed,uid=1000,utf8,umask=077,flush)
/dev/sdd1 on /media/DAVE STICK type vfat (rw,nosuid,nodev,uhelper=hal,shortname=mixed,uid=1000,utf8,umask=077,flush)

```looks like they are vfat, not nfs

and don't they have 'rw' permissions??
Yes, they're vfat (what Windows calls FAT32), so NTFS-3G won't help you. Didn't you say in the thread you linked to that you can delete files? If so, you have write access and your problem is something else.

### Post by yani1shu on 2008-09-14
Well there are two problems with just following the instructions given, 1. i don't have a Windows machine available to use, and 2. the root of the problem is that when i attach the two hard drives to my machine at once, only one shows up under fdisk. It's like the OS isn't able to see the two drives at the same time.

### Post by mssever on 2008-09-15
> **yani1shu said:**
> Well there are two problems with just following the instructions given, 1. i don't have a Windows machine available to use, and 2. the root of the problem is that when i attach the two hard drives to my machine at once, only one shows up under fdisk. It's like the OS isn't able to see the two drives at the same time.
Do you mean that either drive works if plugged in by itself, but you get that error when you plug in the second drive, regardless of the order? That would be quite surprising, since that error normally means the filesystem is dirty, and must be cleaned in Windows.

Of course, if you don't have Windows available, there isn't much of a case for using NTFS, since it's far from an ideal filesystem for Linux. You could do an end run around the problem and reformat them to ext3 or some other Linux-friendly filesystem.

---

### Post by mssever on 2008-12-15
> **jdackle said:**
> Hi givre, this was a very old post of yours but I couldn't find the info I need anywhere else. I think it's a question of nomenclature! :lol:

So it's about the term "setuid-root". What does it mean exactly that a file is setuid-root?
Does it mean it is simply owned by user root OR
Does it mean it is owned by root AND it has the SUID bit turned on?

In case you're wondering, this is where I'm coming from:
[Re: Restricting access to NTFS partition or even folder]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6370023&postcount=6")
Setuid root means that the file is owned by root and has the setuid bit set so normal users can execute the file with root's privileges.

### Post by mssever on 2008-12-17
> **Reset05 said:**
> Hi everyone.

I need help, it s been 2 days i am trying to mount my windows xp partition(hpfs/ntfs) in my new ubuntu 8.10.

I tried a lot of things I read on many forums but without any results(or good ones at least). When I try to mount it with "sudo mount -a", it replies:



here is my output from the command "sudo fdisk -l"



The one I am triyng to mount(winxp) is "/dev/sdb1" and my Ubuntu(8.10) is "/dev/sdb6"
First, this thread is outdated, and you shouldn't follow the instructions in the first post, since you're running Intrepid.

It looks to me like your NTFS partition is somehow corrupt. Try booting into XP and running the disk check utility on the partition. Then reboot into XP again. Then try again from Linux.

Also, please post your /etc/fstab, just in case something's screwy there.

### Post by Reset05 on 2008-12-17
It seem i can't even start winxp anymore. I just re-installed Ubuntu and im doing upgrades right now. I think i m gonna have to format my hard drive and re-install everything again to have it to work well...

But before I format, there is some data i really would like to save from erasing, is there another way i can save specific files or folders in Winxp from Ubuntu?

---

### Post by mssever on 2008-12-17
> **Reset05 said:**
> It seem i can't even start winxp anymore. I just re-installed Ubuntu and im doing upgrades right now. I think i m gonna have to format my hard drive and re-install everything again to have it to work well...

But before I format, there is some data i really would like to save from erasing, is there another way i can save specific files or folders in Winxp from Ubuntu?
Given that your XP partition is apparently hosed, I wouldn't hold out much hope. You can look around for data recovery tools that work with NTFS (you might have to look in the Windows world for this). Unless you can mount the partition, you can't access the data from Ubuntu.

One thing you can do if you have enough available disk space is make a copy of the partition. That would allow you to go ahead and reformat, then if you ever figured out a way to recover your data, you could do so. You can mount files as if they were disks in Linux. I don't know if Windows can do that, though, and it's probable that most data recovery that deal with NTFS will be for Windows. At any rate, to copy an entire partition: ```
dd if=/dev/sdb1 of=/name/of/file/to/save/as bs=512
```This is written from memory, so it might not be quite correct. Read the dd man page to be sure.
